title: Report of the Editor ofThe Journal of Financefor the Year 2024

article.introduction:
I am happy to report that 2024 was another good year forThe Journal of Finance.
I first want to share that the American Finance Association (AFA) board reappointed theJournal’s editorial team to a second term, starting in the summer of 2025. Leonid Kogan, Jonathan Lewellen, Thomas Philippon, and Antoinette Schoar will continue in their current roles. Urban Jermann has decided to step down after the first term ends, in July 2025. We are very sad to lose Urban as our colleague on the editorial team. But I am pleased to announce that Adi Sunderam has agreed to be the new coeditor. Adi is the Willard Prescott Smith Professor of Corporate Finance at Harvard Business School and Research Associate at the NBER. His research interests span corporate finance, asset pricing, and financial intermediation. His expertise will give us additional strength in the areas where we see a very large flow of papers.
Second, I am delighted to announce that we will inaugurate a new Best Paper Prize for Junior Researchers in honor of Rick Green, who was a former editor of theJournaland champion of junior faculty and PhD students. The Rick Green prize will be awarded annually, and paper selection will follow the selection process of the otherJournalprizes. Eligible papers will be those where all researchers are less than seven years out from their PhD graduation at the time of paper submission. The prize money is set at $5,000. Over the last year, we raised over $75,000 of individual donations and pledges as an endowment to finance the prize. The first prize will be awarded in 2026.
Third, the innovations that I announced at the board meeting last year have been successfully implemented. With Wiley's Dryad as our data repository, the data and code-sharing process are now fully up and running. Most of the forthcoming papers, even those accepted before the code-sharing policy was implemented, are following the process. We have seen marked improvements in the quality and clarity of code, which hopefully will facilitate replication and allow researchers to build on each other's work. I am tremendously grateful to the data editor, Hong Ru, for his work on setting up this process. In addition, theJournaleditors held the first webinars with the authors of the Brattle Group and DFA first-prize-winning papers in the summer of 2024. The webinars were well attended and can be accessed on the AFA website.
Submissions to theJournalin 2024 went down from the all-time high of the pandemic years. TableIdetails the number and timing of submissions received throughout the year. We received 1,066 submissions of which 937 were new manuscripts and 129 were resubmissions. For comparison, the number of new submissions was 1,142 in 2023. The reduction in the number of submissions is mostly explained by the increase in theJournalsubmission fees this year (after it had been stable for more than a decade). The quality of the submitted papers remains high.
The average turnaround times increased compared to previous years. This is largely driven by the reduction in submissions, which has also reduced the desk rejection rate. As can be seen in TableII, in 2024, 66% of editorial decisions took less than 70 days (down from 72% in 2023). The median turnaround time in 2023 of 47 days is slightly higher than in 2023 (43 days). Also, the share of decisions that took over 100 days has increased compared to the previous year (14% vs. 8%). Figure1compares turnaround over the 2022 to 2024 period. The overall desk rejection rate in 2024 was 39%, slightly down from 43% in 2023, but slightly higher compared to the previous years. The aim is to spare authors a lengthy review process in cases where we cannot see a reasonable chance that a submitted paper could eventually clear the bar for publication in theJournal.
TableIIIprovides information on editorial decisions. Focusing on initial submissions, the probability of an editor asking for a revision rose to 13.5% (from 11.7% in 2023). The likelihood of a revise-and-resubmit (R&R) conditional on not being desk rejected rose to 16.5%. The total number of R&R rose in 2024 to 152 (from 143 in 2023). Moreover, the number of acceptances in 2024 increased to 84 (from 79 in 2023).
To maintain theJournal'sposition as the premier outlet in the field of finance, our editorial team is determined to ensure an efficient review process for authors. The number of rounds until acceptance is a particularly important metric in this regard. As in previous years, we have made an effort to limit the number of revisions until acceptance. As Figure2shows, among accepted papers in 2024, 52% were accepted after at most one revision; the majority of accepted papers (92%) reached acceptance status after at most two revisions.
TheJournal'svisibility and impact remain very high. As TableIVshows, articles published in theJournalwere cited 49,856 times in 2023. Our two-year impact factor (cites during 2024 to articles published in 2022 and 2023, divided by the total number of articles published in 2021 and 2022) was at 7.6. Our relative ranking based on the two-year impact factor makes theJournalsecond among the finance journals and among the top economics journals. TheAmerican Economic Review (AER)and theQuarterly Journal of Economics (QJE)are the only journals that had a higher impact factor. The five-year impact factor is 10. Here again theJournalranks second among all finance journals, and fifth among all finance and top-five economics journals (only behind theAER,QJE, Journal of Financial Economics (JFE), and Journal of Political Economy (JPE)).
Figure3tracks the total number of papers published in the top-three finance journals. For many years now, both theJFEand theReview of Financial Studieshave published more papers. With 89 articles in 2024, theJournalpublished 17 more articles than in 2023. However, our pipeline of accepted but not-yet-published papers remains very strong. For this reason, we expect to continue this higher publication rate also in 2025.
TableVsummarizes the primary affiliations of the authors that published in theJournalin 2024. In particular, the table shows the number of authors per institution (where an article withnauthors is counted as 1/narticles for each author's institution). The institutions with the mostJournalauthors in 2024 were Stanford in first place, and Yale University and the University of Chicago tied in second place.
We would not be able to run theJournalwithout the help and support of a large number of individuals. First, I want to thank my coeditors Urban Jermann, Jonathan Lewellen, Leonid Kogan, and Thomas Philippon. Throughout the editorial process, our associate editors have provided outstanding advice and guidance. Additionally, last year theJournalrelied on almost 1,000 referees who, with few exceptions, made time in their busy schedules to provide diligent and high-quality feedback that played a vital role in theJournal'ssuccess.
Our outstanding assistant editor Wendy Washburn manages the operational side of theJournal. She handles many of the day-to-day aspects of the submission flow, keeps track of data and metrics, updates our website, and expertly supervises the copyediting and prepublication interface. Wendy has served theJournalfor 22 years and we are immensely grateful to have her on our team. We are also fortunate to have a superb copyeditor, Brenda Priebe. With her extensive experience in copy-editing finance papers, Brenda ensures that articles published in theJournalare readable and accurate.
Finally, each year theJournalawards several prizes for articles that it has published. The papers eligible for the 2024 prizes were all those published in the six issues of theJournalfrom December 2023 through October 2024. The Dimensional Fund Advisors awards recognize articles in all areas except corporate finance, while the Brattle Group awards recognize articles in corporate finance. For both groups, there are first place and distinguished paper prizes. Each group of award winners share prize money totaling $45,000. The associate editors nominated the papers and provided rank-ordered nominations of their top-three papers. The editors made the final selection among the highest-ranked candidates. Assistant editor, Wendy Washburn, handled the balloting. TheAppendixlists the winning papers. On behalf of theJournal, I congratulate all the authors of the nominated papers. The prizes were announced at the AFA Business Meeting.
